cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to work through this question. The question is about what is implied in the passage regarding "deep acting." The options are A through D. First, I need to recall what the passage said about deep acting. From the text, deep acting is contrasted with surface acting (fake smiling). The passage mentions that deep acting involves making people smile by thinking of pleasant memories. Dr. Scott's study found that women were helped more by deep acting, meaning their moods improved more by thinking of pleasant memories. The passage also states that although deep acting can improve moods a little in the short term, it's not a long-term solution. It says that if you do this over a long period, you start to feel unreal and may not feel like yourself anymore. So, the implication here is that while deep acting has some positive short-term effects, there are negative long-term consequences. Now, looking at the options: A) doesn't have any effect on men. The passage doesn't mention anything about men not being affected by deep acting. In fact, it says women were helped more by deep acting, but it doesn't say men aren't affected. So this might not be correct. B) cannot improve our moods in any case. The passage clearly says that deep acting does improve moods in the short term. So this option is incorrect. C) harms our feelings in the long run. The passage does mention that over the long term, deep acting can make you feel unreal and not like yourself.
